pathway_description: High dependence on rainfall with minimal irrigation infrastructure makes agriculture vulnerable to erratic monsoons.
stress_description: Farmers report insufficient water for crop production, particularly for rabi and zaid seasons or during dry spells in kharif.

aer_reference.description: Hot sub-humid to semi-arid ecoregion with coastal alluvium-derived soils

condition.qualitative_description: More than 60% of cropland is under single kharif cropping and cropping intensity is near 1.0, indicating that the vast majority of cultivation is confined to the monsoon season with no meaningful second crop.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In sub-humid to semi-arid coastal plains of AER-18, a high share of single-season kharif area combined with low cropping intensity is a strong indicator of rainfed dependence. Rainfed areas in South Asia — estimated at over 116 million ha in arid/semi-arid tropical systems — are characterised by variable rainfall and limited irrigation access, confining cropping to the monsoon window. Where cropping intensity approaches 1.0, it confirms that farmers cannot sustain a rabi or zaid crop due to absence of stored or diverted water.

condition.qualitative_description: Double-cropped area is less than 20% of total cropland, and surface water body area persisting into the rabi season is very low, confirming that neither irrigation infrastructure nor natural water storage supports post-monsoon cropping.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low double-cropped area directly reflects the inability to sustain a second crop without reliable water supply. In coastal alluvial plains, surface water bodies (tanks, reservoirs, wetlands) that retain water into the rabi season are the primary source of supplemental irrigation. A low swb_rabi_area_ha indicates rapid post-monsoon drawdown, leaving farmers without water for rabi sowing. Together, these two variables confirm structural rainfed dependence rather than a transient drought event.

condition.expression: nrega_irrigation_count < 3 AND canal_name IS NULL or canal_name = 'none'
condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 3 MGNREGA irrigation works recorded in the micro-watershed and no canal infrastructure identified, indicating minimal public investment in irrigation and absence of surface water conveyance.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: The absence of canal infrastructure and low MGNREGA irrigation investment are structural indicators that the area has not received irrigation development attention. Rainfed less-favoured areas in South Asia are characterised by minimum development attention and an unfinished agenda for infrastructure investment. Low NREGA irrigation counts signal that even community-level water harvesting works (farm ponds, check dams, field channels) are absent, amplifying vulnerability to erratic monsoons. The presence of a named canal, by contrast, would suggest partial irrigation access and would weaken the rainfed risk diagnosis.

condition.qualitative_description: Annual runoff exceeds 35% of rainfall in a plains terrain context, indicating high unproductive water loss that could be captured but currently is not, compounding rainfed risk.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: High runoff relative to rainfall in flat coastal plains indicates that a significant fraction of monsoon precipitation is lost as surface flow rather than being stored for crop use. In tropical residual moisture regimes with short intense rainy seasons, unproductive losses via runoff are highest. Plains terrain is amenable to in-situ water harvesting (farm ponds, broad-bed furrows, bunds), meaning high runoff in this terrain type signals a missed opportunity for water retention rather than a biophysical constraint. This amplifies rainfed risk by confirming that water is available in principle but not being captured.

overall_reasoning_note: Rainfed risk in coastal sub-humid plains of AER-18 should be confirmed by at least two of the three primary signals: high single-kharif area with low cropping intensity (sig_01), low double-crop area combined with low rabi surface water body persistence (sig_02), or absence of canal and low NREGA irrigation investment (sig_03). High runoff on plains terrain (sig_04) amplifies the diagnosis but does not confirm it alone. If a canal is present but double-crop area is still low, investigate whether the canal is functional and whether tail-end farmers have access — this may indicate irrigation inequity rather than pure rainfed dependence. Always verify whether low cropping intensity reflects rainfed constraints or post-cyclone land abandonment, which is common in coastal Odisha and Andhra Pradesh.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought causing apparent rainfed risk through reduced monsoon rainfall rather than structural absence of irrigation infrastructure
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare single-kharif area and cropping intensity across multiple years. If low double-cropping is confined to drought years (low annual_rainfall_mm) but recovers in normal years, drought is the primary driver rather than structural rainfed dependence. Structural rainfed risk will show persistently low cropping intensity regardless of rainfall year. Also check canal_name — if a canal exists but is dry due to upstream drought, the pathway is drought-driven, not rainfed infrastructure deficit.

confounders.confounder: Post-cyclone or flood-induced land abandonment mimicking rainfed risk through reduced cropped area and low cropping intensity
confounders.how_to_distinguish: In coastal Odisha and Andhra Pradesh, cyclone events can cause multi-year reductions in cultivated area and cropping intensity that superficially resemble rainfed risk. Check lulc_single_kharif_ha trend for sudden step-change reductions coinciding with known cyclone years. If the reduction is abrupt and followed by partial recovery, the driver is disaster-induced abandonment, not chronic rainfed dependence. Rainfed risk will show a stable, persistently low cropping intensity baseline.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Do you have any data or local knowledge on the net irrigated area in this village or block — for example, from revenue records, the agriculture department, or farmer surveys? Even a rough estimate of what fraction of farmland gets irrigated in a typical year would help.
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Net irrigated area directly quantifies irrigation access. If irrigated area is less than 20% of total cropland, this strongly confirms the rainfed risk pathway. If irrigated area is moderate (20–50%) but double-cropping is still low, the diagnosis may shift toward irrigation inefficiency or tail-end access inequity rather than pure rainfed dependence, changing solution recommendations toward distribution reform rather than new infrastructure.

sources.title: Rainfed Areas and Animal Agriculture in Asia: The Wanting Agenda for Transforming Productivity Growth and Rural Poverty
sources.relevance_note: Documents the biophysical characteristics of rainfed less-favoured areas in South Asia, including the 116 million ha of arid/semi-arid tropical systems in India, characterised by variable rainfall, limited irrigation, and minimum development attention. Directly supports the structural rainfed dependence diagnosis in AER-18 contexts.

sources.title: Management of crop water under drought: a review
sources.relevance_note: Reviews unproductive water losses (runoff, evaporation) across climatic regimes, noting that tropical residual moisture regimes with short intense rainy seasons have the highest runoff losses. Supports the use of annual_runoff_mm as an amplifying signal for rainfed risk and the rationale for in-situ water harvesting on plains terrain.

sources.title: Socio-economic and climate change impacts on agriculture: an integrated assessment, 1990–2080
sources.relevance_note: Provides agro-ecological zoning framework and bio-physical soil and crop growth determinants used to characterise sub-humid and semi-arid zones. Supports the AER-18 context definition and the vulnerability of rainfed systems to erratic monsoons under climate variability.

recommended_solutions: In-situ water harvesting (farm ponds, bunds, broad-bed furrows)
Promotion of short-duration drought-tolerant crop varieties
Rainwater harvesting structures scaled to slope and drainage density
Crop insurance and risk mitigation advisory
